concepts.title: Regression metrics: MSE, RMSE, MAE, MAPE, R²

beats.text: Every regression metric starts from one object: the residual, the gap between each truth and its prediction.
Mean squared error squares each residual, so one error of ten counts as much as a hundred errors of one.
Mean absolute error punishes every unit of error equally, which makes it far more robust to outliers than RMSE.
MAPE rescales each residual by the truth, giving a unit-free percentage you can compare across series of different magnitude.

visual.note: explodes when any y is near zero

beats.text: R-squared is the fraction of variance explained against the dumbest baseline, always predicting the mean.

visual.note: can go negative when worse than the mean

beats.text: On the worked example with residuals all of size one, RMSE equals MAE equals one and R-squared lands at 0.80.
No regression number is neutral; each encodes an opinion about which errors matter and has a regime where it lies.

visual.sub: Report at least two, read the gap

beats.text: Start with ten residuals of size one: MAE and RMSE both read exactly one, in perfect agreement.
Turn one residual into a single bad day of size ten and the two metrics split apart sharply.
MAE crawls only to 1.9 while RMSE leaps to 3.30; the same data, one metric shrugged and the other tripled.
The MAPE trap: a single true value of 0.01 with a 0.49 miss alone contributes a 4900 percent error term.
The professional habit is to never compute MAPE on data with zeros, and to read the RMSE-to-MAE gap as a tail diagnostic.
The entire grammar of classification metrics is built from a single two-by-two table of counts.
A classifier turns a score into a label by comparing it to a threshold, and every prediction lands in one of four cells.

visual.note: every metric is a ratio of these four

beats.text: False positives and false negatives have different costs, so no single number serves every problem.
The threshold is the dial that moves counts between cells; lower it and both true positives and false alarms rise.
You cannot cut false alarms and misses at the same time by moving the threshold; you only trade one for the other.
On a balanced-looking matrix with sixty correct in each diagonal, accuracy is still only 0.60.
Four ratios of the four counts look interchangeable, but choosing the wrong one is how a model ships looking great and fails.
Precision asks how many flagged positives were real; recall asks how many real positives were caught.
F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, high only when both are high.
On a dataset that is ninety-nine percent negative, always predicting negative scores ninety-nine percent accuracy and catches nothing.
That is the accuracy paradox; under imbalance you report precision, recall, F1, or balanced accuracy instead.
For the worked classifier, precision is 0.80 and recall is 0.667, so F1 is pulled down to 0.727 toward the weaker side.
Decision metrics grade the label after thresholding, but throwing away the probability discards information.
Log loss, or binary cross-entropy, grades the probability itself, rewarding confidence only when it is earned.

visual.note: only one term survives per row

beats.text: Predict the truth with probability one and the penalty is zero; hedge at 0.5 and you pay 0.693, the cost of a coin flip.
The penalty is unbounded, so a confident wrong answer climbs steeply as the predicted probability heads toward zero.
The Brier score is the mean squared error of the probabilities, also proper but bounded at one, so it is gentler on outliers.
Log loss assumes calibrated probabilities, the gap the next chapter on ranking and calibration closes.
